title: The Radical, Unclassifiable Art of William Blake
description:
in Art, Books, Poetry |   	July 17th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

William Blake is a house┬Łhold name, or not far from it, but things get more co
body:
in Art, Books, Poetry |   	July 17th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

William Blake is a house┬Łhold name, or not far from it, but things get more com┬Łpli┬Łcat┬Łed when it comes to pro┬Łfes┬Łsion┬Łal descrip┬Łtion. He was a poet, a painter, and a print┬Łmak┬Łer, at least inso┬Łfar as he wrote poet┬Łry, paint┬Łed paint┬Łings, and made prints. But we canŌĆÖt hope to attain even a basic under┬Łstand┬Łing of his lega┬Łcy if we regard him as one man who hap┬Łpened to have the ener┬Łgy to do three dif┬Łfer┬Łent things. In fact, the osten┬Łsi┬Łbly sep┬Ła┬Łrate artis┬Łtic pur┬Łsuits in which he engaged were but three aspects of a uni┬Łfied act of cre┬Łation, result┬Łing in the likes of Songs of Inno┬Łcence and of Expe┬Łri┬Łence┬Āand his illu┬Łmi┬Łnat┬Łed ŌĆ£prophet┬Łic booksŌĆØ: unclas┬Łsi┬Łfi┬Łable works by ŌĆ£the patron saint of unclas┬Łsi┬Łfi┬Łable artists.ŌĆØ
So Evan Puschak, bet┬Łter known as the Nerd┬Łwriter, labels him in his new video above. Blake made books, ŌĆ£which he designed, wrote, etched, col┬Łored, and print┬Łed him┬Łself, using a tech┬Łnique that he invent┬Łed.ŌĆØ They ŌĆ£mix and syn┬Łthe┬Łsize cat┬Łe┬Łgories, and as a result, the art┬Łwork of the late sev┬Łen┬Łteen- and ear┬Łly eigh┬Łteen-hun┬Łdreds did┬ŁnŌĆÖt real┬Łly know what to make of them.ŌĆØ
It did┬ŁnŌĆÖt help that the Roy┬Łal Acad┬Łe┬Łmy of Arts, found┬Łed when Blake was in ado┬Łles┬Łcence, had laid down its own strict aes┬Łthet┬Łic, gener┬Łic, and for┬Łmal stan┬Łdards. Offi┬Łcial┬Łly an engraver and accord┬Łed the low┬Łly sta┬Łtus there┬Łof, Blake devot┬Łed his labors to real┬Łiz┬Łing his elab┬Ło┬Łrate┬Łly idio┬Łsyn┬Łcrat┬Łic visions using images and words togeth┬Łer in ways that no artist had done before.
This choice ŌĆ£to work as mak┬Łer of words, mak┬Łer of images, and cross┬Łbreed┬Łer of both, amount┬Łed to a deci┬Łsion to live in incom┬Łmen┬Łsu┬Łrable neigh┬Łbor┬Łhoods of mean┬Łing,ŌĆØ┬Āwrites┬ĀThe Cam┬Łbridge Com┬Łpan┬Łion to William Blake author Mor┬Łris Eaves as quot┬Łed by Puschak. Blake swam against the cur┬Łrent ŌĆ£of mod┬Łern human under┬Łstand┬Łing, whose bedrock is the prin┬Łci┬Łple of spe┬Łcial┬Łiza┬Łtion.ŌĆØ Today, near┬Łly two cen┬Łturies after his death, that prin┬Łci┬Łple still obtains, and in some ways more rigid┬Łly than ever. But his work remains, from the Songs┬Āand the prophet┬Łic books to his illus┬Łtra┬Łtions of clas┬Łsics like the┬ĀDivine Com┬Łe┬Łdy,┬ĀPar┬Ładise Lost, and even the Bib┬Łli┬Łcal Book of Job, which he read as ŌĆ£the sto┬Łry of a man who believes, mis┬Łtak┬Łen┬Łly, that sal┬Łva┬Łtion requires a slav┬Łish obe┬Łdi┬Łence to words writ┬Łten in a book.ŌĆØ With his┬Ārules, divi┬Łsions, and cat┬Łe┬Łgories, man has cut┬Āreal┬Łi┬Łty apart;┬Āthrough his art, Blake sought to make it whole again.
Relat┬Łed con┬Łtent:
The Rad┬Łi┬Łcal Artis┬Łtic & Philo┬Łsoph┬Łi┬Łcal World of William Blake: A Short Intro┬Łduc┬Łtion
William BlakeŌĆÖs Hal┬Łlu┬Łci┬Łna┬Łto┬Łry Illus┬Łtra┬Łtions of John MiltonŌĆÖs Par┬Ładise Lost
The Oth┬Łer┬Łworld┬Łly Art of William Blake: An Intro┬Łduc┬Łtion to the Vision┬Łary Poet and Painter
William Blake: The Remark┬Łable Print┬Łing Process of the Eng┬Łlish Poet, Artist & Vision┬Łary
Enter an Archive of William BlakeŌĆÖs Fan┬Łtas┬Łti┬Łcal ŌĆ£Illu┬Łmi┬Łnat┬Łed BooksŌĆØ: The Images Are Sub┬Łlime, and in High Res┬Ło┬Łlu┬Łtion
William BlakeŌĆÖs 102 Illus┬Łtra┬Łtions of the Divine Com┬Łe┬Łdy Col┬Łlect┬Łed in a Beau┬Łti┬Łful Book from Taschen
Based in Seoul,┬ĀCol┬Łin┬ĀMarshall┬Āwrites and broad┬Łcasts on cities, lan┬Łguage, and cul┬Łture. HeŌĆÖs the author of the newslet┬Łter┬ĀBooks on Cities┬Āas well as the books┬ĀĒĢ£ĻĄŁ ņÜöņĢĮ ĻĖłņ¦Ć┬Ā(No Sum┬Łma┬Łriz┬Łing Korea) and┬ĀKore┬Łan Newtro.┬ĀFol┬Łlow him on the social net┬Łwork for┬Łmer┬Łly known as Twit┬Łter at┬Ā@colinmarshall.

title: A Walk Through HomerŌĆÖs Odyssey: A Guide to the Epic Before Seeing Christopher NolanŌĆÖs Film
description: YouŌĆÖre gear┬Łing up to see Christo┬Łpher NolanŌĆÖs Odyssey, but you havenŌĆÖt read the Home┬Łr┬Łic work. Or you read it so long ago that it feels lik
body:
YouŌĆÖre gear┬Łing up to see Christo┬Łpher NolanŌĆÖs Odyssey, but you havenŌĆÖt read the Home┬Łr┬Łic work. Or you read it so long ago that it feels like youŌĆÖve nev┬Łer read it at all. No wor┬Łries. Above, Tom Hol┬Łland and Dominic Sand┬Łbrook, the hosts of The Rest Is His┬Łto┬Łry pod┬Łcast, take you through the major plot lines of the Odyssey, unpack┬Łing the lit┬Łer┬Łary and his┬Łtor┬Łi┬Łcal mean┬Łing of the epicŌĆÖs dif┬Łfer┬Łent tales. ItŌĆÖs a good primerŌĆöjust what you need to get ready for one of the big┬Łger cin┬Łe┬Łma releas┬Łes this year. Enjoy!
If you would like to sup┬Łport the mis┬Łsion of Open Cul┬Łture, con┬Łsid┬Łer mak┬Łing┬Āa dona┬Łtion to our site. ItŌĆÖs hard to rely 100% on ads, and your con┬Łtri┬Łbu┬Łtions will help us con┬Łtin┬Łue pro┬Łvid┬Łing the best free cul┬Łtur┬Łal and edu┬Łca┬Łtion┬Łal mate┬Łri┬Łals to learn┬Łers every┬Łwhere. You can con┬Łtribute through Pay┬ŁPal, Patre┬Łon, and Ven┬Łmo (@openculture). Thanks!
Relat┬Łed Con┬Łtent┬Ā
Watch the First Spec┬Łtac┬Łu┬Łlar Film Adap┬Łta┬Łtion of the┬ĀOdyssey┬Ā(1911)
Archae┬Łol┬Ło┬Łgists Think TheyŌĆÖve Dis┬Łcov┬Łered the Old┬Łest Greek Copy of HomerŌĆÖs┬ĀOdyssey: 13 Vers┬Łes on a Clay Tablet
The His┬Łto┬Łry of Ancient Greece in 18 Min┬Łutes: A Brisk Primer Nar┬Łrat┬Łed by Bri┬Łan Cox

title: 'Voices in the Mirror' Honors the Legacy of Pioneering Photographer Gordon Parks
description: From portraits taken around Harlem in the 1940s to assignments for Life magazine to the 1963 March on Washington, Gordon Parks (1912-2006) wielded
body:
From portraits taken around Harlem in the 1940s to assignments for Life magazine to the 1963 March on Washington, Gordon Parks (1912-2006) wielded his camera as a tool for social justice. He captured civil rights activists like Malcom X and Martin Luther King, Jr. in addition to artists and celebrities such as Helen Frankenthaler and Ingrid Bergman. But he may be best known for his candid portraits of families and communities in the segregated South during the era of Jim Crow. All of these and more will be on view in Voices in the Mirror at Jack Shainman Gallery in mid-September, also marking the 20th anniversary of The Gordon Parks Foundation.
Parks was spurred to pursue photography in 1937 after seeing photos taken for the Farm Security Administration (FSA), which held a mission to document American life. ŌĆ£I┬Āsaw that the camera could be a weapon against poverty, against racism, against all sorts of social wrongs,ŌĆØ he said. ŌĆ£I knew at that point I had to have a camera.ŌĆØ In 1942, he became the first Black photographer hired as part of the initiative, introducing him to Washington, D.C., where he noted that ŌĆ£discrimination and bigotry were worse there than any place I had yet seen.ŌĆØ
ŌĆ£Martin Luther King, Jr., Washington, D.C.ŌĆØ (1963), gelatin silver print, 16 x 20 inches 
Among the images included in Voices in the Mirror are seminal portraits like ŌĆ£American Gothic, Washington, D.C.ŌĆØ (1942), which captures a government worker named Ella Watson with a broom and mop. The work nods to American Regionalist painter Grant WoodŌĆÖs likewise iconic ŌĆ£American GothicŌĆØ painting, created 12 years earlier as an ode to American values. ParksŌĆÖ image represented a starkly contrasted reality.
After speaking with Watson about her life and experience in D.C., Parks recalled that it was ŌĆ£so disastrous┬Āthat I felt that I must photograph this woman in a way that would make me feelŌĆöor make the public feelŌĆöabout what Washington, D.C., was in 1942.ŌĆØ He positioned her in front of a flag with a symbolic mop and broom. ŌĆ£I didnŌĆÖt care about what anybody┬Āelse felt,ŌĆØ he said. ŌĆ£ThatŌĆÖs what I felt about America and Ella WatsonŌĆÖs position inside America.ŌĆØ
The exhibition is accompanied by numerous anecdotes and reflections by some of those who appeared in the photos or had close relationships with those who did, such as Malcolm XŌĆÖs daughter, Qubilah Shabazz, and Cora Taylor, who was one of the women standing near a pair of segregated water fountains in ŌĆ£Segregation in the SouthŌĆØ (1956).
Voices in the Mirror opens on September 18 and continues through November 7 in New York. You might also be interested in the works of other FSA photographers who documented the South during the 1930s and 1940s, such as Russell Lee and Marion Post Wolcott.
ŌĆ£American Gothic, Washington D.C.ŌĆØ (1942), gelatin silver print, 16 x 20 inches
ŌĆ£At Segregated Drinking Fountain, Mobile, AlabamaŌĆØ (1956), archival pigment print, 34 x 34 inches
ŌĆ£Untitled, Shady Grove, AlabamaŌĆØ (1956), archival pigment print, 48 x 48 inches
ŌĆ£Untitled, Harlem, New YorkŌĆØ (1963), gelatin silver print, 16 x 20 inches 
ŌĆ£Untitled, AlabamaŌĆØ (1956), archival pigment print, 42 x 42 inches
ŌĆ£Woman and Dog in Window, Harlem, New YorkŌĆØ (1943), gelatin silver print, 34 x 34 inches 
ŌĆ£Mr. and Mrs. Albert Thornton, Mobile, Alabama, 1956 (1956), archival pigment print, 50 x 50 inches
ŌĆ£Store Front, Mobile, AlabamaŌĆØ (1956), archival pigment print, 48 x 48 inches

title: Man Sentenced to 20 Years for Fatally Stabbing NYC Vogue Dancer
description:
News
                Dmitriy Popov killed the Black gay dancer O'Shae Sibley at a Brooklyn gas station in 2023 in a homophobic h
body:
News
                Dmitriy Popov killed the Black gay dancer O'Shae Sibley at a Brooklyn gas station in 2023 in a homophobic hate crime.
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
        
            A photo of late professional dancer O'Shae Sibley attending a class at the Ailey Extension's drop in program (photo Whitney Browne for Ailey Extension, courtesy Ailey Extension)Nearly three years since the attack, Dmitriy Popov was handed a 20-year sentence today, July 16, over the fatal stabbing of Black gay professional dancer O'Shae Sibley at a gas station in Midwood, Brooklyn. The sentencing comes after Popov was convicted of manslaughter as a hate crime in June, as the 2023 stabbing happened after he and a group of friends confronted Sibley's group with homophobic slurs because the dancer was vogueing to music at a gas pump.ŌĆ£OŌĆÖShae Sibley was simply being himself ŌĆö a Black gay man enjoying a summer evening ŌĆö but his mere existence caused this defendant to curse, harass and ultimately kill him,ŌĆØ said Brooklyn District Attorney Eric Gonzalez upon announcing Popov's sentence.Hailing from Philadelphia, 28-year-old Sibley was a performer with the Philadanco professional dance troupe. After moving to Brownsville, Brooklyn in 2020, he became a devoted student at the Alvin Ailey Dance Theater's drop-in classes. Sibley integrated his love of movement with his identity as a proud Black gay man, and in his advocacy for social change and racial justice through public performances and projects directed by his close friend, choreographer Kemar Jewel. After a day at the beach on July 29, 2023, Sibley and his friends stopped to fill gas at a station on Coney Island Avenue after 11 pm. Still clad in swimming trunks, the group began dancing to a Beyonc├® song playing from the car, and Popov's band of friends began taunting Sibley's group with homophobic and racial slurs. The two groups were in a heated argument for a few minutes before ultimately dispersing, but Popov, who was 17 at the time, took out his phone and began filming while reportedly continuing to disparage Sibley and his friends. Sibley turned around and confronted Popov while a witness tried to intervene. He chased Popov to the sidewalk area, where the teenager ultimately pulled out a five-and-a-half-inch blade and stabbed Sibley in the torso. The dancer was pronounced dead after arriving at Maimonides Hospital, and Popov was on the lam for about a week before turning himself in. Popov initially pled not guilty and testified that he acted in self-defense and not hate after Sibley approached again, but failed to convince the jury that convicted him last month.ŌĆ£I hope today's sentence will bring some closure to OŌĆÖShaeŌĆÖs many loved ones and to the entire LGTBQ+ community, which has been shaken by this tragic and deeply upsetting crime,ŌĆØ Gonzalez said. 
                  
                      
                        News
                      
                      
                        New York
                      
                      
                        Brooklyn

title: Pat Oleszko Wins $100K Whitney Biennial Prize
description:
Art Movements
                The artist known for her irreverent inflatables gets the Bucksbaum Award; plus, thereŌĆÖs a new gran
body:
Art Movements
                The artist known for her irreverent inflatables gets the Bucksbaum Award; plus, thereŌĆÖs a new grant for artists living with cancer, Murmurs Gallery closes, and more news.
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
                
        
            Pat Oleszko at Whitney Biennial opening night on March 3, 2026 (┬® BFA 2026, photo Darian DiCianno/BFA.com)
    
        
            Art Movements,┬Āpublished every Thursday afternoon, is a roundup of must-know news, appointments, awards, and other happenings in todayŌĆÖs chaotic art world.Pat Oleszko Gets Whitney Biennial AwardArtist Pat Oleszko, whose oversized inflatable sculpture and early film work dazzled visitors at this year's Whitney Biennial, has received the 2026 Bucksbaum Award. The $100,000 prize is awarded by the Whitney Museum of American Art in recognition of a Biennial artist whose work captures ŌĆ£a singular combination of talent and imagination.ŌĆØ Having just closed out a major solo exhibition at the SculptureCenter in New York City and currently showing works at The Campus in Upstate New York, the artist is having a well-deserved hell of a year.Murmurs Gallery to ShutterThe Los Angeles gallery Murmurs, which mounted shows by the likes of Sula Berm├║dez-Silverman and Alison Nguyen, announced that it is closing its physical space in Downtown LA. ŌĆ£It is no secret that the creative landscape is facing an intense economic squeeze,ŌĆØ its founders wrote. ŌĆ£We are letting go of our space not to stop, but to discover more resilient ways to operate.ŌĆØ They added that a closing celebration is planned for the fall.A New Grant for Artists Living With CancerJacolby Satterwhite (photo Xavier Scott Marshall, courtesy the artist)Two-time cancer survivor Jacolby Satterwhite is the first recipient of the Artists Living with Cancer grant, presented by the Rema Hort Mann Fund and the Robert Rauschenberg Foundation. The uplifting initiative aims to provide much-needed support to visual artists undergoing treatment in the New York metro area. Following this inaugural $25,000 award, the program will dole out one-time grants of up to $10,000.AcquisitionsOrlando Smith, "Aging Is A Fact!" (2025) (image courtesy SFMOMA)The San Francisco Museum of Modern Art (SFMOMA) has acquired over 90 modern and contemporary works from January to June 2026, the institution announced this week, including pieces by Rose B. Simpson, Ana Mendieta, Betye Saar, and Kishio Suga. Among the notable additions to the collection is a watercolor on paper by graphic artist Orlando Smith, who is currently incarcerated at San Quentin prison. "Aging Is A Fact!" (2025), pictured above, draws our attention to the risks and struggles of the prison's aging population.What Else Happened? James Cohan Gallery will now be known as Norr Cohan following the news that gallery partner David Norr has assumed sole ownership of the New York City space. Jeanette Andrews is now represented by Trotter&Sholer gallery.Louis Blue Newby is now represented by Ehrlich Steinberg gallery.
                  
                      
                        Art Movements
                      
                      
                        Community

title: A View From the Easel
description: Welcome to the 346th installment of A View From the Easel, a series in which artists reflect on their workspace. This week, Patrick Guett
body:
Welcome to the 346th installment of A View From the Easel, a series in which artists reflect on their workspace. This week, Patrick Guetta repurposes brand imagery to spread environmentalist messages and approaches the streets of Los Angeles as mini museums in themselves.Want to take part? Check out our┬Āsubmission guidelines┬Āand share a bit about your studio with us through┬Āthis form! All mediums and workspaces are welcome, including your home studio.Patrick Guetta, Los Angeles, CaliforniaHow long have you been working in this space?IŌĆÖve been working in this space for two years. First, I had to build the studio itself and give it energy. Little by little, it transformed completely from an empty space into a place filled with ideas, colors, creation, and dreams.Describe an average day in your studio.I wake up slowly and try not to rush unless I have a meeting. Usually I start around 10 or 11, and I let the day unfold organically. I try not to put boundaries on creativity and let inspiration take over. Sometimes I work late, sometimes I leave and come back, and sometimes answering one email suddenly turns into creating. ItŌĆÖs a very organic process.How does the space affect your work?The space is very important because it feels like home, so IŌĆÖm very comfortable in it. ItŌĆÖs sunny, filled with natural light, very private, and peaceful. That atmosphere helps me disconnect from the outside world and immerse myself in the creative process. Sometimes I can completely lose myself in the work for hours.How do you interact with the environment outside your studio?I can walk practically everywhere and rarely use my car. IŌĆÖm surrounded by Fairfax, Melrose, and Beverly areas filled with graffiti, street art, energy, and creativity. I walk constantly through the neighborhood, to coffee shops, and around the city. ItŌĆÖs a very inspiring place to be in Los Angeles, and I feel lucky to have my studio in the middle of all that energy.What do you love about your studio?What I love most about my studio is that itŌĆÖs mine. I designed it around the way I like to work and live creatively. ItŌĆÖs very private, peaceful, and allows me to fully focus without distractions. It feels personal, comfortable, and completely connected to my energy.What do you wish were different?Honestly, I donŌĆÖt wish anything were different. I feel very lucky. My studio is an extension of my home, and my home is an extension of my studio. Everything feels connected and comfortable for the way I live and create. IŌĆÖm not looking to grow into something bigger right now. I just want the freedom to create peacefully and stay inspired.What is your favorite local museum?I donŌĆÖt really have one favorite museum because each one has a different creative energy. But honestly, one of my favorite museums is the street itself. I love walking through Los Angeles, looking at billboards, signs, graffiti, and visual culture. My work comes from observing what people create, say, and consume. In a way, I turn those everyday surroundings into art through the way I see them.What is your favorite art material to work with?I really like working with acrylic paint because of the vivacity and intensity of the colors. It translates the energy of my work very naturally. I also love photography and use it a lot in my creative process. Sculpture interests me too, but itŌĆÖs a much more complicated process. Acrylic on canvas feels immediate, spontaneous, and connected to the way I like to create.
                  
                      
                        Community
                      
                      
                        A View From the Easel

title: Manhattan DAŌĆÖs Office Returns Three Antiquities to Mexico
description:
News
                The officeŌĆÖs sixth repatriation to the nation included a Nayarit sculpture seized from The Met and an Aztec ston
body:
News
                The officeŌĆÖs sixth repatriation to the nation included a Nayarit sculpture seized from The Met and an Aztec stone previously possessed by a convicted antiquities trafficker.
            
                
                                
                            
                
            
        
            Nayarit artist(s) created this standing male figure around 100ŌĆō400 CE. (image courtesy the Metropolitan Museum of Art)A 1,600-year-old sculpture previously held by the Metropolitan Museum of Art is being repatriated to Mexico, the Manhattan District AttorneyŌĆÖs office announced on Wednesday, July 15. The DA's office seized three archeological objects, which include a ceramic Nayarit sculpture (c. 100ŌĆō400 CE) formerly held in The Met's collection, following new information obtained from the Antiquities Trafficking Unit's ongoing investigations. The other repatriated objects are a Xochipala Bowl (c. 1200ŌĆō900 BCE) seized from New York's Merrin Gallery in 2025 and an obsidian stone core (c. 1000ŌĆō1500 CE) used to make Aztec blades. The latter was formerly under the possession of trafficker Eugene Alexander, who was convicted in 2025 for his role in New York's illicit antiquities trade. The three antiquities possess a collective value of more than $160,000, according to the DA's statement, which noted that it has now returned 52 antiquities to Mexico totaling a value of over $13 million. Recent seizures of objects from The Met have brought the total valuation of looted artifacts surrendered by the museum to $95 million, as reported earlier this month. In a Hyperallergic piece last week, art crime scholar Erin L. Thompson revealed that The Met had repatriated a Roman bust to the Republic of Turkey in February ŌĆö one of many artifacts in US museums acquired via Phoenix Ancient Art, whose co-founder was convicted on charges related to antiquities trafficking. The repatriation of the three Nayarit objects was marked by a ceremony in which Consul General Marcos Bucio represented the government of Mexico and emphasized the country's commitment to defending their cultural sovereignty.ŌĆ£This Nayarit figure is far more than an archaeological object: It is a powerful testimony to the creativity, identity, and collective memory of the peoples of ancient Mexico,ŌĆØ Bucio said in a statement.The Met Director and CEO Max Hollein expressed his gratitude to "the experts and authorities whose work helped clarify the provenance of this object" in a press release. In 2023, The Met instituted their own provenance research team, which has since doubled in size and now regularly works with investigators from the DA's Antiquities Trafficking Unit."We believe that the trafficking networks we have identified are responsible for other pieces looted throughout Mexico," DA Bragg stated in a separate press release. "And our work continues." 
                  
                      
                        News
                      
                      
                        New York
                      
                      
                        Metropolitan Museum of Art
                      
                      
                        Repatriation
                      
                      
                        provenance
